channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: Highguard Players Get Surprise Refunds On PS5 As Studio Shuts Its Doors For Good
item.content:encoded:
Less than a week after Highguard‘s servers shut off for good, the ill-fated hero shooter appears to be issuing refunds, at least on PlayStation 5. The game went offline in record time and Sony is seemingly having Wildlight Entertainment pay back players for any money they invested into it as one last parting gift before the studio shuts its doors for good.

Players who spent money on the free-to-play shooter’s battle pass or skin shop were greeted on March 17 with emails from Sony notifying them that money they’d spent on the game had been returned to their accounts. It’s unclear if everyone who ever spent money on Highguard is getting it back or only those players who invested after Wildlight Entertainment announced layoffs just a few weeks after launch. It’s possible the refunds were mandated by Sony. It’s not clear if refunds are also going out on Steam or Xbox.

Given the game is free to download, refunding everyone would mean the shooter from former Apex Legends and Titanfall developers would, all told, end up earning nothing over its barely two month lifespan.

“I honestly didn’t care for a refund,” wrote one player on the subreddit. “I enjoyed the game and enjoyed supporting it but if you were looking for a refund they are sending them out.” I’m in a similar boat. I played over 30 hours of the game and enjoyed it plenty with my friends. I decided to buy $20 worth of skins as a “thank you” to the developers even after it was clear the game was doomed.

One thing that’s confused people about Highguard‘s rapid flameout is why the studio didn’t have more runway to try and make the game work. Having played a fair bit, it’s easy to feel like the game was rushed out and would have benefited from beta test feedback or additional content at launch. According to Bloomberg, some developers at the studio believed funding from Tencent was clawed back at the last minute when it became clear the game was underperforming at launch.

It seems unlikely that Wildlight Entertainment will continue either at this point. The LinkedIn page was recently removed and the website is currently offline.



https://kotaku.com/highguard-refunds-ps5-steam-xbox-shutdown-2000679761
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Arc Raiders Studio Parts Ways With Cofounder Over Sexual Misconduct Allegations It Was Unable To Substantiate
item.content:encoded:
Embark Studios, maker of The Finals and Arc Raiders, was formed back in 2018 by former veterans of Battlefield maker DICE. It’s now parted ways with one of those cofounders, CCO Rob Runesson, following allegations of an inappropriate relationship with an esports streamer involved with one of the studio’s games. Embark said the decision was mutual despite an outside investigate being unable to substantiate the allegations after they first spread on social media.

“Our leadership team was recently made aware of allegations against an Embark employee,” the studio told IGN in a statement. “We take these matters seriously, and as a part of our process for addressing any types of allegations, we immediately hired a law firm to conduct an external investigation. The investigation did not substantiate the allegations, but we found the situation unsustainable and mutually agreed to part ways with Rob.”

The allegations of an inappropriate relationship with an esports streamer for The Finals were circulated online via multiple Google docs and discussed across various channels and subreddits, IGN reports. An anonymous person claimed a popular female streamer had concerns about a sexual relationship she had with Runesson but was unable to do something about it because he leveraged his stature to promote her channel. However, the female streamer in question later reached out to IGN to say that some of the allegations circulated were out of context or misleading.

Runesson was an art lead at DICE across several well known games, including Mirror’s Edge, Battlefield: Bad Company 2, and Star Wars: Battlefront. He formed Embark along with several fellow former colleagues including Magnus Nordin, Stefan Strandberg, Jenny Huldschiner, and Patrick Söderlund. Runesson most recently served as chief content officer for the Nexon owned studio whose 2025 extraction shooter hit Arc Raiders has sold over 14 million copies. Runesson did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The studio recently addressed other allegations of misconduct within The Finals’ competitive scene between individual players. “For the sake of our community, our players, our partners, and the long term health of our game, we believe it is responsible to temporarily pause our current outbound esports efforts,” Embark posted in The Finals Discord last month. “This season’s competitive model was built with strong intentions, and it is our role to ensure it meets the standards we set.”



https://kotaku.com/rob-runesson-embark-finals-esports-misconduct-allegations-2000679734
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Street Fighter 6’s Latest DLC Ending Is Upsetting Fans Because Alex Marries His Sister-Cousin
item.content:encoded:
All right, I know that headline is probably a lot to take in, but let’s get the background info out of the way first. Earlier today, Street Fighter 6’s latest DLC character, Alex, finally dropped. Fans have been excited to get their hands on Alex for a long time, considering he was the main character in Street Fighter III: New Generation, which probably explains why Street Fighter 6 just broke its all-time concurrent player count peak on Steam.

Fans want to get their hands on Alex for a different reason now, though, as the Arcade Mode ending for the character features a rather questionable trajectory for his ongoing lore: incest.

For context, every new DLC character in Street Fighter 6 gets a little custom slideshow with some new story beats and some unlockable content when you beat Arcade Mode for the first time. In Luke’s ending, he gets into a street fight with Jamie. In Guile’s ending, he attempts to make up with his brother-in-law, Ken. And, in Alex’s ending, he marries and impregnates his second cousin and step-sister, Patricia.

As spotted by Event Hubs, the Street Fighter fanbase is not taking this well. Alex is a fan-favourite character, and a lot of people, myself included, have a bit of an attachment to him, as Street Fighter 3: New Generation was the first game in the series we played. Fun side note: the Arcade Mode ending in that game features a 20-year-old Alex and a 14-year-old Patricia talking to Tom, Patricia’s dad and Alex’s adoptive father, as he lies in a hospital bed. Tom is the first cousin of Alex’s dead mother, by the way.

Some fans are desperately trying their best to explain the resolution in a way that makes it slightly less weird. For instance, fans are currently arguing whether Tom is technically Alex’s adoptive father, as this is apparently only outright stated in ancillary Street Fighter media, such as the UDON Comics. However, as Event Hubs notes in their article, Alex refers to Tom as his father in Street Fighter 6’s World Tour mode.

Also, even if Tom wasn’t Alex’s adoptive father, Alex and Patricia are still literally cousins, so there’s no way to logic your way out of Capcom’s bizarre decision. Hopefully, this isn’t a teaser of what’s to come in Resident Evil Requiem’s future, otherwise Leon’s mysterious wife really will end up being Sherry.



https://kotaku.com/street-fighter-6-alex-dlc-ending-patricia-tom-sister-cousin-married-2000679707
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
